q: Thadingyut is the Lighting Festival of Myanmar. It is the time many Myanmar people decorate the buildings with colourful paper lanterns. (Join the sentences, using an appropriate relative pronoun or relative adverb.) 2024
a: Thadingyut is the Lighting Festival of Myanmar when many Myanmar people decorate the buildings with colourful paper lanterns.

q: Can you bring me the pencils? I left them in the classroom. (Join the sentences, using an appropriate relative pronoun or relative adverb.) 2024
a: Can you bring me the pencils which I left in the classroom?

q: The athlete won the gold medal in the competition. His hard work and dedication are second to none. (Join the sentences, using an appropriate relative pronoun or relative adverb.) 2024
a: The athlete whose hard work and dedication are second to none won the gold medal in the competition.

q: A police officer stopped and arrested the robbers. His car was parked at the next corner. (Join the sentences, using an appropriate relative pronoun / relative adverb.) 2025
a: The police officer whose car was parked at the next corner stopped and arrested the robbers.

q: Thuzar was given a cash award. She won the first prize in the maths quiz competition. (Join the sentences, using an appropriate relative pronoun/ relative adverb.) 2026
a: Thuzar, who won the first prize in the maths quiz competition, was given a cash award.

q: This is a beautiful village. I was born in this village. (Join the sentences, using 'a relative adverb'.) 2026
a: This is a beautiful village where I was born.
